1.1

Circular Flow of Income

The circular flow of income models how money moves continuously between households and firms, providing the essential framework for measuring national income and understanding injections and leakages.

Fig 1: The basic circular flow of income. Households supply factors of production to firms via factor markets, earning income; firms supply goods and services to households via product markets, in exchange for spending.
National Income
The total value of income earned within an economy over a given period — equal, by definition, to the total value of output produced and the total value of expenditure on that output.
Injections
Spending entering the circular flow from outside the household-firm loop: investment (I), government spending (G), and exports (X).
Leakages (Withdrawals)
Income leaving the circular flow rather than being spent domestically: savings (S), taxation (T), and imports (M).
Fig 2: The full four-sector circular flow, adding government, financial markets, and the foreign sector — each contributing an injection into, or a leakage from, the flow of income.
Method of Measuring National IncomeExplanation
Output methodSums the value added by every industry in the economy.
Income methodSums all factor incomes — wages, rent, interest, and profit.
Expenditure methodSums all spending on final goods and services: C + I + G + (X−M).
Why All Three Methods Give the Same Total: every pound of expenditure on a final good becomes income for whoever supplied the factors that produced it, and the value of output produced equals the income generated in producing it — meaning the output, income, and expenditure measures of GDP are, in principle, identical, differing in practice only due to statistical measurement error.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: UK National Accounts
The Office for National Statistics (ONS) publishes UK GDP using all three circular-flow-based measurement approaches (output, income, expenditure) each quarter, reconciling small statistical discrepancies between them — a direct real-world application of the identity that all three measures should, in principle, arrive at the same total national income.
1.2

Aggregate Demand

Aggregate demand (AD) is the total planned expenditure on goods and services produced within an economy, at a given price level.

Aggregate Demand (AD)
AD = C + I + G + (X−M), where C is consumption, I is investment, G is government expenditure, X is exports, and M is imports.
ComponentKey Determinants
Consumption (C)Disposable income, interest rates, consumer confidence, wealth effects, credit availability.
Investment (I)Interest rates, business confidence, expected future demand (the accelerator, Section 5), access to credit.
Government expenditure (G)The economic cycle, automatic stabilisers, political priorities, fiscal constraints.
Net trade (X−M)Domestic and foreign income growth, the exchange rate, relative inflation rates, non-price competitiveness.
Why the AD Curve Slopes Downward: unlike an individual demand curve, this reflects three economy-wide effects: the real balance effect (a higher price level reduces the real value of wealth, reducing consumption), the interest rate effect (a higher price level raises money demand, pushing up interest rates and reducing interest-sensitive spending), and the international trade effect (a higher domestic price level makes exports less competitive and imports more attractive).
The Role of Expectations: consumption and investment both depend heavily on expected future conditions, not just current income — rising unemployment expectations increase precautionary saving and reduce current consumption even before any actual income fall occurs, while pessimistic business expectations can depress investment even when current interest rates are low.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The 2008 Financial Crisis and AD Collapse
The 2008 financial crisis triggered a sharp fall in UK aggregate demand as consumer confidence collapsed, credit became scarce, and both consumption and investment fell simultaneously — illustrating how multiple AD components can move together during a systemic shock, rather than in isolation.
1.3

Aggregate Supply

Aggregate supply (AS) shows the relationship between the price level and total output firms are willing to supply, with fundamentally different behaviour in the short run versus the long run.

Short-Run Aggregate Supply (SRAS)
The total output firms are willing to supply at each price level, assuming input costs (especially wages) remain fixed — SRAS slopes upward.
Long-Run Aggregate Supply (LRAS)
The total output an economy can produce once wages and prices have fully adjusted, reflecting the economy's underlying productive potential.
Cause of a Shift in SRASEffect
Wage costsHigher wages shift SRAS left; lower wages shift SRAS right.
Commodity pricesA rise in oil/commodity prices shifts SRAS left (cost-push inflation, Section 9).
Exchange rateA weaker currency raises import costs, shifting SRAS left.
Fig 3: The neo-classical (Classical) view of LRAS. LRAS is vertical at potential output, reflecting the assumption that wages and prices are fully flexible in the long run, so the economy always tends back towards full employment output whatever happens to AD in the short run.
Diagram pending — to be added The Keynesian LRAS curve: rather than the vertical Classical line above, Keynesians argue LRAS is elastic (flat) at low output with spare capacity, becoming increasingly steep and eventually vertical only near full capacity — reflecting "sticky" wages that resist falling even when demand is weak, meaning an economy can settle below full employment for a prolonged period rather than self-correcting automatically.
FeatureNeo-Classical (Classical) ViewKeynesian View
Shape of LRASVertical at potential output, at every output levelElastic at low output; increasingly steep near full capacity
Wage flexibilityFully flexible — markets clear automatically"Sticky" — wages resist falling
Policy implicationFavours free-market, supply-side solutionsFavours active demand-side intervention during a downturn
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The 2021–22 Energy Price Shock
A sharp rise in global gas and oil prices following Russia's invasion of Ukraine raised firms' energy costs across the UK economy, shifting SRAS left and contributing directly to the cost-push inflation examined in Section 9 — a clear real-world illustration of a supply-side shock.
1.4

The Interaction of Aggregate Demand and Supply

Bringing AD and AS together determines macroeconomic equilibrium — and the assumptions underlying each side of the model shape how that equilibrium responds to shocks.

Macroeconomic Equilibrium
The price level and output at which AD equals AS — no inherent tendency for either to change further, though this need not occur at full employment.
Fig 4: A negative output gap. AD intersects SRAS to the left of LRAS, leaving the economy producing below potential output, easing inflationary pressure and raising cyclical unemployment.
AssumptionExplanation
Ceteris paribusAll other variables are held constant while analysing one specific change.
Rational agentsHouseholds and firms are assumed to act to maximise utility and profit respectively.
Model simplificationReal economies involve far more interacting variables than the AD/AS model can capture; it is a simplified representation, not a complete description.
Evaluating the Effects of AD/AS Changes on Macroeconomic Indicators: a rightward AD shift raises output and the price level in the short run (Section 9's demand-pull inflation) and can reduce unemployment (Section 8) — but if AD growth persistently outpaces LRAS growth, the economy moves into a sustained positive output gap, risking accelerating inflation (Section 13's Phillips Curve). A rightward LRAS shift (successful supply-side policy, Section 16) allows higher output at a lower price level than would otherwise be possible, easing the growth-inflation trade-off.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The COVID-19 Negative Output Gap
UK real GDP fell by around a fifth in the second quarter of 2020 as lockdown restrictions closed large parts of the economy, opening the largest negative output gap in decades — actual output fell far below potential output almost overnight, illustrating how quickly AD/AS equilibrium can shift following a major shock.
1.5

The Multiplier and the Accelerator

The multiplier describes how an initial change in injections produces a proportionately larger final change in national income; the accelerator links investment to the rate of change of demand.

The Multiplier
The ratio of the final change in national income to the initial change in injections that caused it.
Marginal Propensity to Consume/Save/Withdraw (MPC/MPS/MPW)
The proportion of an additional pound of income spent domestically (MPC), saved (MPS), or withdrawn from the circular flow via saving, tax, or imports combined (MPW = MPS + MPT + MPM).

Worked Example — Calculating the Multiplier

MPC = 0.6. Government increases spending by £10bn.

Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1−MPC) = 1 ÷ 0.4 = 2.5
Final change in national income = £10bn × 2.5 = £25bn
Factor Affecting Multiplier SizeEffect
Marginal propensity to withdrawHigher MPW produces a smaller multiplier.
Spare capacityThe multiplier tends to be larger during a recession with spare capacity, since firms can expand output without triggering inflation.
The Accelerator
The theory that the level of investment depends on the rate of change of national income or demand, not its absolute level — if growth is steady, firms invest merely to maintain capacity; if the growth rate slows, even while demand is still rising, investment can fall sharply.
Why the Accelerator Makes Investment Volatile: because investment responds to the change in demand rather than its level, small fluctuations in the growth rate of consumer spending can produce much larger swings in investment — a key reason investment is typically far more volatile than consumption or GDP itself over the trade cycle, and a mechanism that can amplify the multiplier's effect on the cycle.
Cause of an Output GapExplanation
Demand shockA sudden change in C, I, G, or (X−M) shifts AD, moving actual output away from potential (Section 4).
Supply shockA sudden change in costs or productivity shifts SRAS, moving actual output relative to potential.
Consequences of an Output Gap: a positive output gap risks accelerating inflation as the economy "overheats"; a negative output gap involves lost output and rising cyclical unemployment (Section 8) — both the multiplier and accelerator help explain how an initial shock can generate output gaps considerably larger than the shock itself.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The IMF's 2012 Multiplier Re-Estimate
The IMF's October 2012 World Economic Outlook found that fiscal multipliers during the 2010–12 period had been considerably larger (around 1.5) than originally assumed (around 0.5) when designing post-2008 austerity programmes — meaning each £1 of spending cuts contracted the economy by roughly £1.50, substantially more than forecast.
2.1

Economic Growth

Economic growth — the increase in an economy's real output over time — is a core macroeconomic policy objective, moving through recognisable phases known as the economic (trade) cycle.

Economic Growth
An increase in a country's real output (real GDP) over time.
Real GDP
GDP adjusted for inflation, allowing genuine comparison of output over time.
GDP Per Capita
GDP divided by population — a better measure of average living standards than GDP alone, since it accounts for population size.

Worked Example — Real GDP and Growth Rate

Nominal GDP rises from £2,200bn to £2,310bn; the GDP deflator rises from 100 to 105.

Real GDP (Year 2) = £2,310bn ÷ 1.05 = £2,200bn
Real GDP growth = (£2,200bn − £2,200bn) ÷ £2,200bn = 0%

Key point: despite 5% nominal growth, real growth was zero once inflation is stripped out.

Stage of the Economic CycleCharacteristics
BoomHigh growth, low unemployment, rising inflationary pressure — a positive output gap.
Downturn/RecessionFalling or negative growth, rising unemployment.
SlumpOutput well below potential, high unemployment — a negative output gap.
RecoveryGrowth resumes, unemployment begins to fall.
Type of GrowthCause
Short-run (actual) growthUsing previously idle resources more fully, closing a negative output gap.
Long-run (potential) growthAn increase in the quantity or quality of factors of production, shifting LRAS right.

Benefits of Economic Growth

  • Rising average incomes and living standards.
  • Higher tax revenue at a given tax rate.
  • Lower cyclical unemployment.
  • Improved government finances relative to GDP.

Costs of Economic Growth

  • Environmental costs — resource depletion, pollution, emissions.
  • Risk of demand-pull inflation if growth persistently outpaces potential.
  • Can worsen inequality if benefits are unevenly distributed.
  • Structural change can generate concentrated regional unemployment.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The UK's Productivity Puzzle
UK productivity growth has been notably weak since the 2008 financial crisis compared with the pre-crisis trend, a phenomenon widely termed the "productivity puzzle" — despite low unemployment, output per worker has grown far more slowly than historical patterns would predict, directly limiting the UK's long-run potential growth (Section 6).
2.2

Development

Economic development is broader than growth alone, encompassing the structural transformation of an economy and the policy objective of sustainable, lasting improvement in living standards.

Primary Sector
Economic activity extracting raw materials directly from nature — agriculture, mining, fishing, forestry.
Secondary Sector
Economic activity transforming raw materials into manufactured goods — manufacturing, construction, processing.
Tertiary Sector
Economic activity providing services — retail, finance, education, healthcare, tourism.
The Structural Transformation Pattern: as economies develop, employment and output typically shift from the primary sector towards the secondary sector, and eventually towards the tertiary sector — the UK, for example, has seen its primary sector (agriculture, mining) shrink to a small fraction of output, its manufacturing base decline relative to its historic peak, and services grow to dominate the modern economy. Many developing economies remain heavily weighted towards the primary sector, a structural feature closely linked to the barriers to development and Prebisch-Singer terms-of-trade dynamics examined in the companion BIZ-OMICS Economic Development and Growth guide.
Sustainable Development
Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs — balancing economic growth against environmental and resource constraints.
The Relationship Between Growth and Sustainable Development: economic growth and sustainable development can conflict where growth relies on rapid depletion of natural resources or environmental degradation, but they need not always conflict — "green growth" strategies aim to decouple rising output from rising environmental damage through cleaner technology and resource efficiency. Whether a specific growth path is sustainable depends on the resources and methods it relies upon, not on its measured growth rate alone.
MeasureWhat It CapturesLimitation
GDP/GNI per capitaAverage income.Says nothing about distribution, health, education, or sustainability.
Human Development Index (HDI)Combines income with life expectancy and education.Still excludes inequality and environmental sustainability.
Alternative social/cultural indicatorsE.g. the Genuine Progress Indicator, adjusting for inequality and environmental damage.Less standardised, harder to compare consistently across countries.
Cross-Reference: the full detail of development classifications (LEDC/MEDC/NIC), barriers to development, the Prebisch-Singer condition, Dutch Disease, and development strategies is covered in depth in the companion BIZ-OMICS Economic Development and Growth guide, and in Section 23's treatment of the Harrod-Domar model and microfinance.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: China's Structural Transformation
Since market-oriented reforms began in 1978, China has undergone one of history's most rapid structural transformations, shifting hundreds of millions of workers from primary-sector agriculture into secondary-sector manufacturing and, increasingly, tertiary-sector services — a live illustration of the structural transformation pattern examined in this section, achieved over decades rather than centuries.
2.3

Employment

Full employment is a core macroeconomic objective, but measuring unemployment accurately requires distinguishing genuinely involuntary joblessness from other forms of economic inactivity.

Employment
People of working age who are in paid work.
Unemployment
People of working age without a job, but actively seeking and available for work.
UK MeasureMethodologyKey Limitation
Claimant CountCounts people claiming unemployment-related benefits.Excludes unemployed people not eligible for or claiming benefits.
Labour Force Survey (LFS)A household survey asking whether respondents are actively seeking and available for work.Internationally comparable, but subject to sampling error.
Type of UnemploymentCause
FrictionalShort-term unemployment between jobs.
StructuralA mismatch between workers' skills/location and available jobs.
CyclicalCaused by a fall in AD during a downturn (a negative output gap).
Real-wageCaused by a wage held above the market-clearing equilibrium.

Effects of Full Employment

  • Higher output and tax revenue.
  • Reduced benefit spending.
  • Risk of labour shortages pushing up wage inflation if pursued too aggressively.

Consequences of Unemployment

  • Lost output below potential.
  • Higher fiscal cost (benefits, lower tax revenue).
  • Hysteresis — long-term unemployment can erode skills, raising the natural rate of unemployment itself.
  • Social costs — poorer health outcomes, higher poverty.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The UK Furlough Scheme
The UK government's Coronavirus Job Retention Scheme (furlough), introduced in 2020, paid a substantial share of wages for employees unable to work due to lockdown restrictions, directly preventing the kind of cyclical unemployment (Section 8) that would otherwise have resulted from the sudden collapse in demand — at its peak, the scheme supported millions of jobs simultaneously.
2.4

Inflation

Price stability — low and predictable inflation — is a core macroeconomic objective, measured through official price indices.

Inflation
A sustained rise in the general price level.
Deflation
A sustained fall in the general price level.
Disinflation
A fall in the rate of inflation — prices still rising, but more slowly.
Hyperinflation
Extremely rapid, out-of-control inflation, typically eroding confidence in a currency entirely.
UK MeasureKey Feature
Consumer Prices Index (CPI)The Bank of England's official 2% inflation target measure.
Retail Prices Index (RPI)Includes housing costs (mortgage interest) that CPI excludes; increasingly viewed as flawed.

Worked Example — Calculating Inflation Using Index Numbers

A price index rises from 108 to 114 over one year.

Rate of inflation = (114 − 108) ÷ 108 × 100 = 5.6%
Fig 5: Demand-pull inflation. AD shifts right along SRAS — both price level and output rise together.
Fig 6: Cost-push inflation. SRAS shifts left while AD stays unchanged — price level rises but output falls, producing stagflation.
Cost of InflationExplanation
Erosion of real incomeFixed incomes lose purchasing power.
Menu costsReal resource cost of updating prices.
International competitivenessHigher domestic inflation than trading partners worsens competitiveness (Section 21).
UncertaintyMakes business planning more difficult.
Causes and Consequences of Deflation: deflation can be "good" (driven by falling costs/productivity, e.g. technology) or "bad" (driven by collapsing demand) — bad deflation risks a deflationary spiral, as consumers delay purchases expecting further price falls, further depressing demand.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The 2021–2023 UK Inflation Spike
UK CPI inflation rose from close to the 2% target in early 2021 to over 11% by late 2022, driven substantially by the energy price shock (Section 3) following Russia's invasion of Ukraine and continuing global supply chain disruption — a clear real-world example of cost-push inflation (Fig. 6) overwhelming the Bank of England's inflation target.
2.5

Balance of Payments

The balance of payments is a record of all financial transactions between a country's residents and the rest of the world, and a sustainable position is a core macroeconomic objective.

Balance of Payments
A record of all economic transactions between a country's residents and the rest of the world, split into the current account and the capital/financial account.
Current Account
Records trade in goods, trade in services, primary income (wages, interest, profits, dividends), and secondary income (transfers such as remittances and foreign aid).
Fig 7: The four components of the current account. Trade in goods and services make up the "trade balance"; adding primary and secondary income gives the overall current account balance.

Worked Example — Calculating the Current Account Balance

Trade in goods: −£30bn. Trade in services: +£25bn. Primary income: −£5bn. Secondary income: −£3bn.

Current account balance = −30 + 25 − 5 − 3 = −£13bn (a deficit)
Assessing a Current Account DeficitConsideration
Cyclical vs structuralA deficit tied to strong domestic demand pulling in imports is less concerning than one reflecting a long-run competitiveness problem.
What it financesA deficit financing investment (importing capital goods) is generally less concerning than one financing pure consumption.
How it is financedStable long-term FDI inflows are more sustainable than volatile short-term "hot money" flows.
Exam Application: never treat a current account deficit as automatically "bad" or a surplus as automatically "good" — always assess the cause, what it is financing, and how sustainably it is being funded before reaching an evaluative judgement.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The UK Services Surplus
The UK has run a persistent deficit in trade in goods for decades, partially offset by a substantial and growing surplus in trade in services — particularly financial and professional services centred on the City of London — illustrating how the overall current account balance can mask very different underlying trends in its component parts.
2.6

Trends in Macroeconomic Indicators

Assessing UK economic performance requires looking beyond a single year's figures to identify longer-run trends, and comparing the UK against other developed, emerging, and developing economies.

Trend
The underlying long-run direction of an economic indicator once short-term fluctuations and one-off shocks are stripped out.
Benchmarking
Assessing an economy's performance by comparison against other economies at a similar stage of development, rather than against its own history alone.
IndicatorBroad UK Trend Over the Last Two Decades
Economic growthModest average growth by historic standards, with a severe contraction during the 2008 financial crisis and a further sharp contraction during the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic, followed by uneven recovery.
InflationGenerally close to the 2% target for much of the 2010s, followed by a sharp rise from 2021–2023 driven by energy price shocks and supply chain disruption.
UnemploymentFell substantially from post-financial-crisis highs to historically low levels by the late 2010s, before rising again during the pandemic and subsequently moderating.
Balance of paymentsA persistent current account deficit in most years, partially offset by a strong services (particularly financial services) surplus.
Productivity growthNotably weak since the 2008 financial crisis compared with the pre-crisis trend — the UK's widely discussed "productivity puzzle."
ComparisonConsideration
UK vs other developed economiesUK growth and productivity performance has generally lagged some comparable advanced economies since 2008, a widely debated policy concern.
UK vs emerging economiesEmerging economies such as China and India have typically grown substantially faster than the UK in percentage terms, reflecting their earlier stage of development and greater scope for catch-up growth (companion Economic Development and Growth guide).
UK vs developing economiesThe UK's far higher starting income level and developed institutions mean direct growth-rate comparisons can be misleading without also considering absolute living standards and development indicators (Section 7).
Why Trend Analysis Matters More Than Single-Year Snapshots: a single year's GDP or inflation figure can be heavily distorted by temporary shocks (the pandemic, an energy price spike) — assessing genuine underlying economic performance requires looking at trends over multiple years and business cycles, and comparing outcomes against economies at a similar stage of development, rather than drawing conclusions from any single data point in isolation.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The UK vs G7 Growth Comparison
UK GDP growth has broadly tracked, but at times lagged, other G7 advanced economies since the 2008 financial crisis — a comparison regularly cited in UK economic policy debate, illustrating why trend analysis relative to comparable economies, rather than the UK's own history in isolation, is central to properly assessing macroeconomic performance.
2.7

Income Distribution and Welfare

Beyond the level and growth of national income, how that income is distributed across the population is itself a distinct macroeconomic policy objective and a source of ongoing evaluative debate.

Income Inequality
The extent to which income is unevenly distributed across a population.
Wealth Inequality
The extent to which accumulated assets (property, savings, shares) are unevenly distributed — typically more unequal than income.
Absolute Poverty
Income below a fixed real-terms threshold representing the minimum needed for basic necessities.
Relative Poverty
Income below a fixed proportion (commonly 60%) of contemporary median income.
Fig 8: The Lorenz curve. The diagonal represents perfect equality; the bowed curve shows the actual cumulative distribution of income — the further it bows from the diagonal, the more unequal the distribution.
Fig 9: The Gini coefficient, calculated as Area A ÷ (Area A + Area B) — 0 represents perfect equality, 1 represents perfect inequality, allowing numerical comparison of inequality between countries or over time.
Cause of Poverty and InequalityExplanation
Unemployment and low payDirectly reduces household income.
Skill-biased technological changeRaises demand for high-skilled labour while reducing demand for routine work, widening wage gaps.
Unequal access to educationPerpetuates income differences across generations.
Wealth concentration and inheritanceExisting wealth compounds and transmits across generations, widening wealth gaps faster than income gaps.
Cross-Reference: the full UK-specific treatment of income and wealth inequality, poverty measurement, the Gini coefficient calculation method, and redistribution policy is covered in depth in the companion BIZ-OMICS Income, Wealth and Poverty guide.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The UK Gini Coefficient Over Time
The UK's income Gini coefficient rose significantly during the 1980s before broadly stabilising at a historically elevated level since the 1990s — official ONS statistics tracking this trend are widely used in the ongoing UK policy debate over whether income inequality has meaningfully narrowed or simply plateaued.
2.8

The Phillips Curve

The Phillips Curve models the relationship between unemployment and inflation — the clearest formal illustration of a policy conflict between two macroeconomic objectives.

Natural Rate of Unemployment
The rate of unemployment that persists once cyclical unemployment is eliminated, reflecting frictional and structural factors alone.
NAIRU (Non-Accelerating Inflation Rate of Unemployment)
The rate of unemployment consistent with stable, non-accelerating inflation.
Fig 10: The Short-Run Phillips Curve (SRPC). As unemployment falls, inflation rises, accelerating sharply near the NAIRU — the classic short-run trade-off.
Fig 11: The expectations-augmented Phillips Curve. At the same NAIRU, higher expected inflation shifts the whole SRPC upward — the position of the curve depends on expected inflation, not unemployment alone.
Why the Original Phillips Curve Broke Down: the 1970s combination of high inflation and high unemployment (stagflation) contradicted the simple Phillips Curve, which predicted this combination should be nearly impossible — the expectations-augmented model explains this by showing that once expected inflation adjusts upward, unemployment returns to the NAIRU but at a permanently higher inflation rate, meaning attempts to hold unemployment below the NAIRU only work temporarily.
Fig 12: Has the Phillips Curve gone flat? A flatter curve observed in many economies in recent decades shows inflation rising much more gradually as unemployment falls below the NAIRU, suggesting the trade-off may have weakened.
The Usefulness of the Phillips Curve for Policymakers: the model remains a useful framework for thinking about the short-run inflation-unemployment trade-off, but its practical usefulness for precise policy-setting is limited by an unobservable NAIRU, an unstable relationship that appears to shift over time (Fig. 12), and the fact that supply-side shocks (Section 16) can shift the curve itself rather than simply moving the economy along it.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: 1970s UK Stagflation
The UK experienced severe stagflation during the 1970s — simultaneously high inflation (peaking above 20%) and high unemployment — following oil price shocks and rising wage-price spirals, a combination the original Phillips Curve predicted should be almost impossible, and a key episode that discredited the simple original model in favour of the expectations-augmented version (Fig. 11).
3.1

Fiscal Policy

Fiscal policy — government spending and taxation — is a primary tool for managing aggregate demand and pursuing macroeconomic objectives.

Government Budget
The government's plan for spending and taxation over a fiscal year.
Budget Deficit/Surplus/Balanced Budget
A deficit occurs when spending exceeds revenue (requiring borrowing); a surplus when revenue exceeds spending; a balanced budget when the two are equal.
Discretionary Fiscal Policy
Deliberate policy decisions to change spending or taxation.
Automatic Stabilisers
Spending and tax changes that occur automatically with the economic cycle, without new policy decisions (e.g. benefit spending rising automatically during a downturn).
Tax TypeDefinition
Direct taxLevied directly on income or wealth (e.g. income tax).
Indirect taxLevied on spending (e.g. VAT).
Progressive taxAverage rate rises as income rises.
Proportional taxAverage rate stays constant regardless of income.
Regressive taxAverage rate falls as income rises.
Fig 13: The three tax structures compared by average rate of tax paid as income rises.
Fig 14: The Laffer Curve. Revenue is zero at both 0% and 100% tax rates, rising then falling in between as higher rates increasingly discourage work, investment, and honest reporting of income.

Worked Example — Average and Marginal Tax Rates

A taxpayer earns £40,000, paying £6,000 tax on the first £30,000 and 40% on the remaining £10,000.

Additional tax on last £10,000 = £4,000
Total tax = £6,000 + £4,000 = £10,000
Average tax rate = £10,000 ÷ £40,000 = 25%
Marginal tax rate on the last pound earned = 40%
Cyclical Budget Position
The portion of the budget balance attributable to the current position in the economic cycle.
Structural Budget Position
The portion of the budget balance that would persist even at full employment — reflecting a genuine ongoing mismatch between spending and revenue.
Crowding Out
Government borrowing raising interest rates in the loanable funds market, reducing private investment.
Fig 15: The loanable funds market. Government borrowing adds to demand for funds, pushing up the interest rate and potentially crowding out private investment.
National Debt
The total accumulated stock of government borrowing built up over time.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: UK Austerity, 2010–2019
Following the 2008 financial crisis, UK governments pursued a sustained programme of spending cuts and tax changes aimed at reducing the budget deficit — a real-world test of fiscal multiplier theory (Section 5) that generated extensive debate over whether the pace of consolidation was appropriate given the size of the negative output gap at the time.
3.2

Monetary Policy

Monetary policy — interest rates and the money supply — is set independently by the Bank of England's Monetary Policy Committee to manage aggregate demand and inflation.

Monetary Policy
The use of interest rates and the money supply, set by the central bank, to influence aggregate demand and achieve the inflation target.
Bank Rate
The interest rate set by the Bank of England's Monetary Policy Committee, directly influencing the rates commercial banks charge borrowers and pay savers throughout the economy.
Monetary Policy Committee (MPC)
The Bank of England committee responsible for setting Bank Rate independently of government, tasked with meeting the 2% CPI inflation target.
Monetary transmission mechanism (Bank Rate cut): Bank Rate falls → commercial banks reduce mortgage, loan and credit rates → borrowing becomes cheaper and saving less attractive → consumption and investment rise → aggregate demand rises → the multiplier (Section 5) amplifies the effect → demand-pull pressure increases, moving inflation towards target.
ToolMechanism
Interest rate changesDirectly affects the cost of borrowing and return on saving, influencing C and I.
Money supply changesExpanding the money supply can lower interest rates and support lending.
Inflation rate targetsProvide a clear, credible anchor for policy and public expectations.
Quantitative easingThe central bank purchases financial assets to inject money directly into the economy when interest rates are already very low.
Exchange rate influenceInterest rate changes affect capital flows and the exchange rate, which in turn affects net trade (Section 19).
Evaluating Monetary Policy Effectiveness: monetary policy operates with a time lag (typically 18–24 months for the full effect), can be less effective in a liquidity trap where interest rates are already near zero, and its transmission depends on how much household and firm behaviour actually responds to rate changes — a genuinely contested empirical question, not a mechanical certainty.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The Bank of England's 2009 QE Launch
Facing Bank Rate already near zero during the 2008–09 financial crisis, the Bank of England launched quantitative easing in March 2009, purchasing government bonds to inject money directly into the economy — a real-world example of monetary policy operating through an unconventional tool once the standard interest rate channel had been exhausted.
3.3

Supply-Side Policy

Supply-side policy aims to increase an economy's long-run productive potential directly, shifting LRAS rather than managing AD.

Supply-Side Policy
Government policy intended to increase the quantity or quality of factors of production, raising long-run aggregate supply and potential output.
Free-Market Supply-Side Policy
Policy that removes barriers and improves incentives for firms and workers without direct government spending (e.g. deregulation, privatisation).
Interventionist Supply-Side Policy
Policy that directly funds improvements in the quantity or quality of factors of production (e.g. education, infrastructure, R&D spending).
TypeExamples
Free-market supply-side policiesPrivatisation, deregulation, subsidies to specific industries, competition policy, reforms to the tax and benefit system, improved labour market flexibility, immigration control.
Interventionist supply-side policiesInvestment in infrastructure, education, training, and research and development.
Fig 16: A successful supply-side policy shifts LRAS right. With AD unchanged, the new equilibrium allows higher output at a lower price level than before.
Fig 17: Interventionist supply-side spending has a dual effect — it raises AD in the short run (the spending itself, amplified by the multiplier) and raises LRAS later, once the investment matures into extra productive capacity.
Evaluating Supply-Side Policy: supply-side policies typically take far longer to affect the economy than demand-side policy, limiting their usefulness for managing a short-run downturn; interventionist policy can be expensive; and effectiveness depends heavily on the specific type of unemployment or capacity constraint being addressed (Section 8).
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: 1980s UK Supply-Side Reform
The UK government pursued extensive free-market supply-side reform during the 1980s, including large-scale privatisation of state-owned industries, deregulation of financial markets, and reforms to trade union law — widely credited with reshaping the UK's long-run productive capacity, though its short-term costs (including significant regional unemployment) remain a subject of ongoing evaluation.
3.4

Policy Conflicts

The macroeconomic objectives examined throughout Section 2 frequently cannot all be achieved simultaneously — policy conflicts and trade-offs are an unavoidable feature of macroeconomic management.

Policy Conflict
A situation in which pursuing one macroeconomic objective necessarily worsens another, so that a single policy cannot achieve both simultaneously.
Trade-Off
The sacrifice of one objective required to achieve more of another, given limited policy tools and competing goals.
Classic Policy ConflictExplanation
Growth vs inflationStimulating growth via demand-side expansion risks pushing the economy into a positive output gap and demand-pull inflation.
Unemployment vs inflationThe Phillips Curve trade-off (Section 13) — expansionary policy to reduce unemployment risks higher inflation.
Growth vs the balance of paymentsFaster domestic growth increases import spending, potentially worsening the current account (Section 10).
Growth vs the environmentGrowth often carries environmental costs conflicting with sustainability objectives (Section 7).
Fiscal policy vs monetary policyExpansionary fiscal policy funded by borrowing can push up interest rates (crowding out, Section 14), potentially working against an accommodative monetary stance.
Fig 18: How supply-side policy can ease the unemployment-inflation conflict. Successful supply-side policy shifts the long-run Phillips curve left, allowing a lower rate of unemployment to be sustained at the same long-run inflation rate.
Exam Application: the strongest evaluative answers distinguish between short-run conflicts (movements along the Phillips Curve, generally unavoidable) and the possibility of easing these conflicts in the long run through successful supply-side policy — reaching a reasoned judgement about how achievable this is in practice, rather than asserting either that all objectives are always in conflict, or that supply-side policy straightforwardly solves every trade-off.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The UK Cost-of-Living Crisis, 2022
The 2022 UK cost-of-living crisis starkly illustrated the growth-inflation policy conflict examined in this section — the Bank of England needed to raise interest rates to control inflation (Section 9), while government simultaneously introduced substantial energy subsidies to protect households, highlighting the tension between demand-side tightening and supporting living standards during a supply-driven inflation shock.
4.1

International Trade

International trade allows countries to specialise and exchange goods and services, with patterns shifting substantially over recent decades.

International Trade
The exchange of goods and services across national borders.
Trend in the Pattern of International TradeExplanation
Rising share of trade involving emerging economiesChina, India, and other emerging economies have captured a rapidly growing share of world trade as their productive capacity has developed.
Growth of global supply chainsComponents increasingly cross multiple borders during production before final assembly.
Growing trade in servicesDigital technology has made previously "untradeable" services increasingly tradeable across borders.

Advantages of International Trade

  • Allows specialisation according to comparative advantage, raising total global output.
  • Access to larger markets allows economies of scale.
  • Greater choice and typically lower prices for consumers.
  • Can bring capital, technology, and jobs to developing economies via export-oriented growth.

Disadvantages of International Trade

  • Structural unemployment in industries unable to compete with lower-cost foreign producers.
  • Developing economies can remain trapped in low-value primary commodity exports (Prebisch-Singer condition, companion Economic Development and Growth guide).
  • Increased exposure to global economic shocks and volatile commodity prices.
Why the Advantages and Disadvantages Differ by Development Level: developed economies typically capture more of trade's benefits through exporting higher-value manufactured goods and services, while some developing economies remain more exposed to the disadvantages — particularly commodity-dependent economies facing the long-run terms of trade deterioration examined in the companion Economic Development and Growth guide.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The UK-China Trade Relationship
UK trade with China has grown substantially over recent decades as China's economy has developed (Section 7), with the UK importing large volumes of manufactured goods while exporting financial services and other high-value tertiary-sector output — an illustration of two economies at different stages of development trading according to their respective comparative advantages.
4.2

Exchange Rates

An exchange rate is the price of one currency in terms of another, determined differently under floating and fixed exchange rate systems.

Floating Exchange Rate
An exchange rate determined entirely by market demand and supply for the currency, with no central bank intervention.
Fixed Exchange Rate
An exchange rate pegged by government/central bank policy, defended through active intervention.
Fig 19: Equilibrium in the market for a floating currency, set purely by demand for and supply of the currency.

Worked Example — Using Exchange Rates

The exchange rate is £1 = $1.25. A UK exporter sells a good for £800.

Dollar price = £800 × 1.25 = $1,000

Key point: if sterling appreciates to £1 = $1.40, the same good now costs $1,120 in the US market — reducing UK export competitiveness without any change in the underlying sterling price.

Fig 20: Defending an overvalued fixed rate. Excess supply of the currency must be absorbed by the central bank buying its own currency, spending foreign reserves.
SystemKey AdvantageKey Disadvantage
FloatingAutomatic adjustment towards balance of payments equilibrium; frees monetary policy for domestic objectives.Can be volatile, creating uncertainty for trade and investment.
FixedCertainty for international trade and investment; can anchor domestic inflation expectations.Requires holding reserves; sacrifices independent monetary policy.
Causes and Consequences of Exchange Rate Changes: a depreciation/devaluation makes exports cheaper and imports dearer, potentially improving the trade balance (subject to the Marshall-Lerner condition and J-curve, Section 20) but risking imported inflation; an appreciation/revaluation has the reverse effects, improving purchasing power for imports but harming export competitiveness.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Black Wednesday, 1992
On 16 September 1992 ("Black Wednesday"), the UK was forced to withdraw sterling from the European Exchange Rate Mechanism after failing to defend its fixed exchange rate against sustained speculative selling, despite raising interest rates sharply in a single day — a dramatic real-world illustration of the difficulty of defending an overvalued fixed exchange rate (Fig. 20) once markets lose confidence in the peg's sustainability.
4.3

Globalisation

Globalisation — growing economic interdependence between countries — rests on the theory of comparative advantage and shapes international competitiveness worldwide.

Globalisation
The increasing economic integration and interdependence of national economies through growing trade, capital, labour, and information flows across borders.
Absolute Advantage
A country has absolute advantage in a good if it can produce more of it than another country using the same resources.
Comparative Advantage
A country has comparative advantage in a good if it can produce it at a lower opportunity cost than another country — the foundation of the case for trade, even without absolute advantage.

Worked Example — Comparative Advantage

Country A can produce 100 cloth or 50 wine; Country B can produce 60 cloth or 40 wine (using the same fixed resources).

A's opportunity cost of 1 cloth = 0.5 wine; B's = 0.67 wine → A has comparative advantage in cloth
A's opportunity cost of 1 wine = 2 cloth; B's = 1.5 cloth → B has comparative advantage in wine

Key point: both countries gain by specialising and trading, even though A has absolute advantage in both goods.

Terms of Trade
The ratio of a country's export prices to its import prices.

Worked Example — Terms of Trade

Export price index rises from 100 to 108; import price index rises from 100 to 120.

Terms of Trade Index = (108 ÷ 120) × 100 = 90 (deteriorated)
Marshall-Lerner Condition
A devaluation improves the trade balance only if the sum of PED for exports and imports exceeds 1 — otherwise, a devaluation can worsen the trade balance.
Fig 21: The J-curve. The trade balance worsens immediately after devaluation (inelastic short-run demand), then improves as demand becomes more elastic over time, once buyers and sellers can fully respond.

Benefits of Globalisation

  • Exploiting comparative advantage raises global productive efficiency.
  • Access to larger markets and economies of scale.
  • FDI can bring capital and technology to developing economies.

Costs of Globalisation

  • Structural unemployment in industries unable to compete internationally.
  • Increased inequality within and between countries.
  • Loss of domestic policy autonomy as governments compete for mobile investment.
The Impact of Emerging Economy Performance on Other Economies: the rapid growth of major emerging economies affects the rest of the world through commodity demand (raising global commodity prices), competitive pressure on manufacturing (companion Economic Development and Growth guide), and shifting global trade and capital flow patterns — meaning other economies' growth prospects are increasingly linked to the performance of large emerging economies, not just their own domestic conditions.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: China's WTO Accession, 2001
China's accession to the World Trade Organisation in 2001 dramatically accelerated its integration into the global trading system, contributing to a rapid expansion in global trade volumes and reshaping global manufacturing supply chains — a landmark real-world case study of globalisation's scale and pace when a major economy is newly and fully integrated into world trade.
4.4

Trade Policies and Negotiations

Countries can restrict trade through protectionism, or pursue deeper integration through trading blocs — with the World Trade Organisation overseeing multilateral liberalisation.

Protectionism
Government measures restricting international trade to protect domestic industries.
Fig 22: The effect of a tariff. Raises domestic price, reduces imports, increases domestic production, generates tariff revenue — but creates a net welfare loss.
Fig 23: The effect of an import quota — similarly restricts imports and raises price, but the resulting revenue typically accrues to importers/foreign exporters rather than the domestic government.
Type of Trading BlocKey Feature
Free Trade AreaNo tariffs between members; each sets its own external tariff.
Customs UnionNo tariffs between members, plus a common external tariff.
Monetary UnionA shared currency and unified monetary policy.
Economic UnionDeep integration including free movement of capital and labour, harmonised regulation, and often a shared currency.
Fig 24: Trade diversion. A country inside a trading bloc may switch from a genuinely lower-cost external producer to a higher-cost bloc member, purely because of the bloc's common external tariff — a net welfare loss relative to genuine free trade.
Trade Creation
Shifting consumption from a less efficient domestic producer to a more efficient bloc-member producer — a genuine welfare gain.
Trade Diversion
Shifting consumption from a more efficient non-member producer to a less efficient bloc-member producer, a net welfare loss.
The Role of the WTO: the World Trade Organisation oversees global trade rules, negotiates multilateral trade liberalisation, and adjudicates trade disputes between member countries, aiming to promote free trade at a global level rather than only within regional blocs.

Case For Free Trade

  • Maximises global output via comparative advantage.
  • Lower prices and greater choice for consumers.

Case For Protectionism

  • Infant industry protection until competitive.
  • Protecting strategic industries and domestic employment.
  • Countering unfair trade practices such as dumping.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Brexit and UK-EU Trade Barriers
The UK's departure from the EU Customs Union introduced new customs checks, rules-of-origin requirements, and non-tariff barriers to trade with the EU, its largest trading partner, while simultaneously allowing the UK to independently negotiate new trade agreements with non-EU countries — illustrating that leaving a trading bloc involves complex trade-offs, not simply a mirror image of the benefits of joining one.
5.1

Money and Interest Rates

Money underpins the entire financial system, and understanding how it is created and measured is essential to understanding how interest rates are determined.

Functions of Money
A medium of exchange, a unit of account, a store of value, and a standard for deferred payment.
Narrow Money
The most liquid forms of money — cash and instant-access deposits.
Broad Money
Narrow money plus less liquid forms of savings, such as time deposits.
Characteristic of Good MoneyExplanation
DurabilityMust not physically degrade quickly.
DivisibilityMust be divisible into smaller units for transactions of different sizes.
PortabilityMust be easy to carry and use.
AcceptabilityMust be widely accepted in exchange for goods and services.
The Fisher Equation of Exchange: MV = PT, where M is the money supply, V is the velocity of circulation (how often each unit of money is spent), P is the price level, and T is the volume of transactions — implying that, holding V and T constant, an increase in the money supply leads directly to a proportional increase in the price level, a foundational monetarist argument linking money supply growth to inflation.
Money Creation
Commercial banks create money by extending loans — when a bank lends, it credits the borrower's account with a new deposit, expanding the money supply beyond the central bank's own issued currency.
How Interest Rates Are Determined: the Bank of England's Monetary Policy Committee sets Bank Rate based on its assessment of the outlook for inflation relative to its target, directly influencing the rates commercial banks charge borrowers and pay savers throughout the economy (companion Monetary Policy guide's full transmission mechanism, Section 15).
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: Bank of England Rate Rises, 2022–2023
The Bank of England raised Bank Rate repeatedly from near-zero in late 2021 to over 5% by mid-2023, the fastest tightening cycle in decades, directly targeting the inflation spike examined in Section 9 — a clear real-world application of the interest rate transmission mechanism, though its full effect on the economy operated with the substantial time lag discussed in this section.
5.2

The Financial Sector

Beyond facilitating everyday transactions, the financial sector plays a foundational role in channelling savings towards productive investment — a role of particular importance for economic development.

Role of the Financial Sector
Channels funds from savers to borrowers, enabling investment, facilitating risk management, and providing the payments infrastructure that underpins all other economic activity.
Role of Savings and Investment in DevelopmentExplanation
Capital accumulationSavings channelled into investment build the capital stock needed to raise future productive capacity.
The "vicious circle of poverty"Low incomes in developing economies leave little surplus for saving, restricting domestic investment and limiting future income growth — a self-reinforcing trap without external capital or intervention.
The Harrod-Domar Model
An early formal growth model proposing that a country's growth rate depends on its savings ratio divided by its capital-output ratio: Growth Rate = Savings Ratio ÷ Capital-Output Ratio.

Worked Example — The Harrod-Domar Model

A country saves 20% of its national income. Its capital-output ratio is 4 (it takes £4 of capital to produce £1 of additional annual output).

Growth Rate = Savings Ratio ÷ Capital-Output Ratio = 20% ÷ 4 = 5%

Key point: raising the savings ratio, or lowering the capital-output ratio (using capital more efficiently), directly raises the predicted growth rate — the model's central policy implication.

Limitations of the Harrod-Domar Model: the model assumes a fixed capital-output ratio and largely ignores the role of technology, human capital, and institutional quality in determining growth — meaning it can substantially overstate the growth achievable from savings and investment alone, without complementary improvements in these other areas.
MicrofinanceOCR
The provision of small loans, savings accounts, and other basic financial services to low-income individuals and small businesses who lack access to conventional banking — typically because they cannot offer the collateral or credit history conventional lenders require.
How Microfinance Addresses the Financial Sector's Role in DevelopmentExplanation
Bridging the savings-investment gapProvides small-scale capital directly to entrepreneurs and households excluded from the formal financial sector, addressing the Harrod-Domar savings constraint at the grassroots level.
Supporting small business formationSmall loans can fund the working capital needed to start or expand a small enterprise, supporting income growth and employment at the local level.
Financial inclusionBrings previously "unbanked" populations into the formal financial system, potentially improving their ability to save, insure against risk, and access further credit over time.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The Grameen Bank
The Grameen Bank, founded in Bangladesh by Muhammad Yunus (awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2006 partly for this work), pioneered modern microfinance by lending small amounts to poor, often rural borrowers without requiring conventional collateral, relying instead on group lending structures where borrowers support and monitor one another — demonstrating that very poor households could be creditworthy when given access to appropriately designed small-scale finance.

Case For Microfinance

  • Reaches populations conventional banking excludes entirely.
  • Can fund productive small business investment directly.
  • Has supported financial inclusion and, in some contexts, measurable poverty reduction.

Case Against / Limitations of Microfinance

  • Loan sizes are typically too small to fund larger-scale, higher-productivity investment.
  • Some borrowers have faced problematic debt burdens from poorly regulated microfinance schemes.
  • Evidence on its aggregate impact on economic growth (as opposed to individual household outcomes) remains genuinely mixed.
5.3

Financial Regulation

Because financial market failure can impose severe costs on the wider economy, financial institutions are subject to more extensive regulation than most other industries.

UK Regulatory BodyRole
The Bank of EnglandSets monetary policy and holds overall responsibility for UK financial stability.
Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA)Regulates individual banks and insurers to ensure adequate capital and liquidity.
Financial Policy Committee (FPC)Monitors and addresses systemic risk across the financial system as a whole.
Financial Conduct Authority (FCA)Regulates the conduct of financial firms towards consumers and markets.
Why Regulation Is Needed
Financial markets are prone to asymmetric information, moral hazard, and systemic risk (where one institution's failure cascades across the wider system) — market failures with potentially severe consequences for the whole economy if unaddressed.
Post-Crisis Regulatory Reform: following the 2008 financial crisis, regulators significantly raised capital and liquidity requirements for banks, introduced more rigorous stress testing, and separated retail from investment banking activities — reforms directly addressing the "too big to fail" moral hazard problem exposed so severely in 2008.
International InstitutionRole
International Monetary Fund (IMF)Provides financial assistance and policy advice to countries facing balance of payments crises, and monitors global financial stability.
The World BankProvides long-term development finance and policy advice, particularly to developing economies (companion Economic Development and Growth guide).
Evaluating Financial Regulation's Effectiveness: regulation must weigh the systemic risk it protects against against genuine costs — compliance costs for firms, the risk of regulatory capture (where regulators become too closely aligned with the industry they oversee), and the possibility that regulation simply pushes risk-taking into less-regulated parts of the financial system rather than eliminating it.
★ REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE: The 2008 Crisis and Northern Rock
The UK bank Northern Rock experienced a visible bank run in September 2007 — the first in over a century — as depositors queued to withdraw funds after the bank revealed it needed emergency Bank of England support, a direct real-world illustration of the maturity transformation vulnerability underlying the regulatory reforms discussed in this section; Northern Rock was subsequently nationalised, and RBS and Lloyds Banking Group received substantial taxpayer-funded bailouts during the wider 2008 crisis.
BIZ-OMICS | biz-omics.co.uk | OCR A-Level Economics | Component 2: Macroeconomics
